topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physical, Chemical and Biological Characteristics of Water

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Introduction to Water Quality Characteristics

Water quality is defined as the chemical, physical, biological, and radiological characteristics of water relative to the requirements of one or more biotic species or to any human need or purpose. It is a measure of the condition of water in relation to its suitability for drinking, irrigation, industrial use, recreation, and ecosystem sustenance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA), water quality characterization is the **fundamental baseline data** required for any project (dams, industries, townships). The characteristics are broadly classified into **Physical, Chemical, and Biological** categories as per Standard Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater.

### 1.2 Classification of Water Quality Parameters

The overall assessment of water quality is a multidimensional evaluation that integrates three principal domains of analysis:

| Domain | What it Measures | Typical Example Parameters |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Characteristics** | Visible and sensory properties | Turbidity, Color, Odor, Taste, Temperature, TDS, TSS |
| **Chemical Characteristics** | Molecular and ionic composition | pH, Hardness, Alkalinity, DO, BOD, COD, Chlorides, Nitrates |
| **Biological Characteristics** | Living organisms and pathogens | Total Coliforms, Fecal Coliforms, MPN, Pathogens, Algae |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — "The Three-Lens Camera":**
Imagine you are a forensic scientist examining a glass of water. To fully understand its story, you need to:
- **Physical lens (Naked Eye):** You see its color, clarity, temperature, and smell — these are the "first impressions."
- **Chemical lens (Microscope + Reagents):** You dip test strips and use instruments to measure pH, dissolved oxygen, and hardness — these are the "chemical fingerprints."
- **Biological lens (Petri Dish + Microscope):** You culture the water to see which bacteria or algae are present — these are the "living inhabitants."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard):** Water quality is **not a single parameter** but a **composite index** of physical, chemical, and biological attributes that determine its fitness for intended use. Drinking water standards (IS 10500:2012) and effluent discharge standards (IS 2490) prescribe permissible limits for each parameter.

### 1.4 Why Water Quality Matters in EIA

- **Baseline Study:** Required to establish pre-project environmental status.
- **Impact Prediction:** Helps predict post-project degradation (e.g., thermal pollution from a power plant affects DO).
- **Mitigation Design:** Selection of treatment technology depends on these characteristics.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** CPCB/SPCB enforce discharge standards based on these parameters.

### 1.5 Standards and Reference Benchmarks

- **IS 10500:2012** — Indian Standard Drinking Water Specification
- **WHO Guidelines for Drinking Water Quality**
- **BIS 2490 / 3306 / 7968** — Effluent discharge standards
- **APHA Standard Methods** — Reference analytical procedures
- **CPCB Designated Best Use** — Classification of water bodies (A to E)
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Physical Characteristics of Water

Physical parameters are those which can be perceived by human senses or measured without altering the chemical nature of water.

#### 2.1.1 Turbidity
- **Definition:** Turbidity is the cloudiness or haziness of a fluid caused by large numbers of individual particles that are generally invisible to the naked eye, similar to smoke in air.
- **Cause:** Suspended matter such as clay, silt, organic matter, algae, and other microscopic organisms.
- **Measurement:** Measured in **Nephelometric Turbidity Units (NTU)** using a **Nephelometer** based on the **Jackson Candle Turbidimeter** principle.
- **Standard:** IS 10500 permissible limit is **1 NTU (max 5 NTU)**.
- **Why important:** High turbidity shields pathogens from disinfectants, increases treatment cost, and affects aquatic photosynthesis.

#### 2.1.2 Color
- **True Color:** Caused by dissolved substances (e.g., tannins, humic acids). Measured after filtration.
- **Apparent Color:** Includes suspended matter. Measured on unfiltered sample.
- **Unit:** **Hazen Units (HU)** or **Platinum-Cobalt Units (Pt-Co)**.
- **Standard:** ≤ **5 Hazen Units** for drinking water.

#### 2.1.3 Odor and Taste
- Caused by organic compounds, dissolved gases (H₂S, CH₄), chlorination by-products, or industrial effluents.
- Measured organoleptically using the **Threshold Odor Number (TON)**.

#### 2.1.4 Temperature
- Critical because it affects **DO saturation**, biological reaction rates, and aquatic life.
- Thermal pollution from industries (power plants, steel mills) reduces DO and disrupts ecosystems.

#### 2.1.5 Total Solids (TS)
Total solids is the sum of all dissolved and suspended solids in water.

$$TS = TDS + TSS$$

- **TSS (Total Suspended Solids):** Filterable residue retained on a 1.5 µm filter, dried at **103–105 °C**. Unit: **mg/L**.
- **TDS (Total Dissolved Solids):** Solids passing through the filter. Measured by evaporating filtrate. Unit: **mg/L**.
- **Standard:** TDS ≤ **500 mg/L (desirable), 2000 mg/L (max permissible)**.

#### 2.1.6 Electrical Conductivity (EC)
- **Definition:** Measure of water's ability to conduct electricity; proportional to dissolved ion concentration.
- **Unit:** **µS/cm (microsiemens per centimeter)** or **µmho/cm**.
- **Conversion:** TDS (mg/L) ≈ 0.55 × EC (µS/cm)  *(approximately for natural waters)*

### 2.2 Chemical Characteristics of Water

#### 2.2.1 pH (Hydrogen Ion Concentration)
- Negative logarithm of hydrogen ion activity.
- **Standard:** **6.5 to 8.5** (IS 10500).
- **pH < 7:** Acidic (corrosive, affects fish life).
- **pH > 7:** Alkaline (bitter taste, soap-like feel).

#### 2.2.2 Alkalinity
- Ability of water to neutralize acids. Primarily due to **bicarbonates (HCO₃⁻), carbonates (CO₃²⁻), and hydroxides (OH⁻)**.
- **Unit:** mg/L as **CaCO₃**.
- **Standard:** ≤ **200 mg/L** desirable, **600 mg/L** max permissible.

#### 2.2.3 Hardness
- Caused by divalent cations, mainly **Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺**.
- **Total Hardness (TH) = Calcium Hardness + Magnesium Hardness**

$$TH = 2.5 \times [Ca^{2+}] + 4.1 \times [Mg^{2+}] \quad (\text{as mg/L as CaCO}_3)$$

| Classification | TH (mg/L as CaCO₃) |
|---|---|
| Soft | 0 – 60 |
| Moderately Hard | 61 – 120 |
| Hard | 121 – 180 |
| Very Hard | > 180 |

#### 2.2.4 Dissolved Oxygen (DO)
- **Definition:** Amount of gaseous oxygen dissolved in water. Essential for aquatic life.
- **Unit:** **mg/L** or **% saturation**.
- **Standard:** ≥ **5 mg/L** for aquatic life; **6 mg/L** desirable for drinking.
- **DO Saturation (at sea level, 1 atm):**
  - 0 °C → 14.6 mg/L
  - 20 °C → 9.1 mg/L
  - 30 °C → 7.5 mg/L

#### 2.2.5 Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)
- **Definition:** Amount of dissolved oxygen required by aerobic microorganisms to decompose organic matter in water at a specified temperature (usually **20 °C**) over a specified period (usually **5 days**).
- **Standard:** BOD₅ of surface water < **3 mg/L**; sewage typically **100–400 mg/L**.
- **Formula (BOD exerted at time t):**
$$BOD_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k \cdot t}\right)$$

  Where:
  - $L_0$ = Ultimate BOD (mg/L)
  - $k$ = Deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹), typically **0.1 to 0.3**
  - $t$ = Time in days

#### 2.2.6 Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)
- **Definition:** Amount of oxygen required to chemically oxidize the organic and inorganic matter in water using a strong oxidant (typically **K₂Cr₂O₇** in acidic medium).
- **Unit:** mg/L O₂.
- **Advantage:** Test results in **2–3 hours** (vs 5 days for BOD).
- **Relationship:**
$$BOD_5 \approx 0.68 \times COD \quad (\text{for domestic sewage, approx.})$$

#### 2.2.7 Chlorides
- Common in natural waters; high levels indicate sewage or industrial pollution.
- **Standard:** ≤ **250 mg/L** (desirable), **1000 mg/L** (max).

#### 2.2.8 Fluorides
- Beneficial in small amounts (**0.6–1.2 mg/L**) for dental health.
- **> 1.5 mg/L** causes **fluorosis (skeletal and dental)**.

#### 2.2.9 Nitrates and Nitrites
- **NO₃⁻ > 45 mg/L** causes **methemoglobinemia (blue baby syndrome)** in infants.
- Indicates recent organic pollution.

#### 2.2.10 Heavy Metals
- Lead (Pb), Mercury (Hg), Cadmium (Cd), Arsenic (As), Chromium (Cr).
- **Standard:** Pb ≤ 0.01 mg/L, Cd ≤ 0.003 mg/L, As ≤ 0.01 mg/L.

### 2.3 Biological Characteristics of Water

#### 2.3.1 Total Coliforms
- Group of gram-negative, rod-shaped bacteria that ferment lactose at **35–37 °C** within **48 hours**, producing acid and gas.
- **Indicator organism** for fecal pollution.
- **Standard:** **0 MPN/100 mL** (drinking water, IS 10500).

#### 2.3.2 Fecal Coliforms / E. coli
- Subset of total coliforms; grow at **44.5 °C**.
- Direct evidence of recent fecal contamination.

#### 2.3.3 Most Probable Number (MPN)
- Statistical method for estimating bacterial density using multiple tube fermentation.
- **Unit:** MPN per 100 mL.

#### 2.3.4 Other Pathogens
- Bacteria: *Vibrio cholerae*, *Salmonella typhi*, *Shigella*
- Viruses: Rotavirus, Hepatitis A
- Protozoa: *Giardia lamblia*, *Entamoeba histolytica*
- Helminths: *Ascaris lumbricoides*

#### 2.3.5 Biological Indicators
- **BOD itself** is a biological parameter (oxygen demand of microbes).
- **Algal blooms** indicate eutrophication (excess N, P).
- **Bioindicators:** *Daphnia*, fish, macroinvertebrates for stream health.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Unit | Standard (IS 10500) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Solids | $TS = TDS + TSS$ | mg/L | — |
| TDS to EC | $TDS \approx 0.55 \times EC$ | mg/L : µS/cm | TDS ≤ 500 |
| Total Hardness | $TH = 2.5[Ca^{2+}] + 4.1[Mg^{2+}]$ | mg/L as CaCO₃ | ≤ 200 |
| BOD at time t | $BOD_t = L_0(1 - e^{-kt})$ | mg/L | < 3 (surface water) |
| BOD remaining | $L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ | mg/L | — |
| BOD₅ / Ultimate BOD | $BOD_5 = L_0(1 - e^{-5k})$ | mg/L | — |
| COD | Measured via K₂Cr₂O₇ | mg/L | — |
| BOD–COD Ratio | $BOD_5 \approx 0.68 \times COD$ | dimensionless | — |
| DO Saturation | Empirical formula (APHA) | mg/L | ≥ 5 |
| pOH | $pH + pOH = 14$ | — | 6.5–8.5 |
| Langelier Saturation Index (LSI) | $LSI = pH_{actual} - pH_{saturation}$ | — | LSI = 0 (balanced) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These parameters form the **decision matrix** for designing water treatment plants (WTP) and sewage treatment plants (STP). For example, high BOD requires biological treatment (activated sludge, trickling filter), high TDS requires Reverse Osmosis, and high turbidity requires coagulation-flocculation-sedimentation.

### 2.5 Summary of Physical, Chemical, Biological Test Methods

| Parameter | Method/Instrument | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Turbidity | Nephelometer | IS 3025 (Part 10) |
| TDS | Conductivity meter / Gravimetric | IS 3025 (Part 16) |
| pH | pH meter (glass electrode) | IS 3025 (Part 11) |
| Hardness | EDTA titration | IS 3025 (Part 21) |
| DO | Winkler method / DO meter | IS 3025 (Part 38) |
| BOD | BOD incubator, 5-day, 20 °C | IS 3025 (Part 44) |
| COD | Open reflux / Closed reflux | IS 3025 (Part 58) |
| Coliforms | MPN / Membrane filtration | IS 1622 |
| Fluoride | SPADNS / Ion-selective electrode | IS 3025 (Part 60) |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: BOD Exerted at Time t (First-Order Kinetics)

The biochemical oxidation of organic matter in water follows **first-order reaction kinetics** with respect to the remaining biodegradable organic matter.

**Step 1:** Rate of oxygen uptake is proportional to the remaining organic matter at time t.

$$\frac{dL_t}{dt} = -k \cdot L_t$$

Where:
- $L_t$ = BOD remaining (oxygen demand yet to be satisfied) at time t (mg/L)
- $k$ = deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹)

**Step 2:** Rearrange the equation to separate variables for integration.

$$\frac{dL_t}{L_t} = -k \cdot dt$$

**Step 3:** Integrate both sides from initial condition (t = 0, $L_t = L_0$) to (t, $L_t$).

$$\int_{L_0}^{L_t} \frac{dL_t}{L_t} = -k \int_0^t dt$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integrals.

$$[\ln L_t]_{L_0}^{L_t} = -k \cdot [t]_0^t$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the boundary values.

$$\ln L_t - \ln L_0 = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 6:** Apply logarithm rules to combine.

$$\ln \left(\frac{L_t}{L_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 7:** Exponentiate both sides to obtain the remaining BOD at time t.

$$L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

**Step 8:** The BOD **exerted** up to time t is the difference between the ultimate BOD and the remaining BOD.

$$BOD_t = L_0 - L_t$$

**Step 9:** Substitute the expression for $L_t$ from Step 7.

$$BOD_t = L_0 - L_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

**Step 10:** Factor out $L_0$ to obtain the final standard BOD equation.

$$\boxed{BOD_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k \cdot t}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This equation shows that BOD exerted increases asymptotically toward the ultimate BOD $L_0$. The rate constant $k$ (typically 0.1–0.3 per day for domestic sewage at 20 °C) controls how fast oxygen is consumed.

### 3.2 Derivation: Relation Between BOD₅ and Ultimate BOD (L₀)

**Step 1:** Use the BOD equation from Step 10 above with t = 5 days.

$$BOD_5 = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k \cdot 5}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for $L_0$ in terms of BOD₅.

$$L_0 = \frac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}$$

**Step 3:** This is the **standard dilution equation** used in BOD lab analysis. If $k = 0.23 \, \text{day}^{-1}$ (typical), then:

$$e^{-5 \times 0.23} = e^{-1.15} \approx 0.3166$$

$$L_0 = \frac{BOD_5}{1 - 0.3166} = \frac{BOD_5}{0.6834} \approx 1.46 \cdot BOD_5$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples

#### Example 1: Calculating Total Hardness

**Given:** Ca²⁺ = 80 mg/L, Mg²⁺ = 24 mg/L
**Find:** Total Hardness as CaCO₃

**Step 1:** Apply the total hardness formula.

$$TH = 2.5 \times [Ca^{2+}] + 4.1 \times [Mg^{2+}]$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values (the factors 2.5 and 4.1 convert the cation concentrations to equivalent CaCO₃).

$$TH = 2.5 \times 80 + 4.1 \times 24$$

**Step 3:** Calculate each term separately.

$$TH = 200 + 98.4$$

**Step 4:** Sum the terms to obtain the final answer.

$$TH = 298.4 \, \text{mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 5:** Classify the water. With TH = 298.4 mg/L, the water is classified as **Very Hard**.

#### Example 3.2: Calculating BOD Exerted

**Given:** $L_0 = 300 \, \text{mg/L}$, $k = 0.23 \, \text{day}^{-1}$, $t = 5$ days
**Find:** BOD₅ and BOD remaining

**Step 1:** Calculate $e^{-k \cdot t}$.

$$e^{-0.23 \times 5} = e^{-1.15} = 0.3166$$

**Step 2:** Compute BOD₅ using the derived equation.

$$BOD_5 = 300 \times (1 - 0.3166) = 300 \times 0.6834 = 205.02 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 3:** Compute BOD remaining at t = 5.

$$L_5 = 300 \times 0.3166 = 94.98 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 4:** Verify the relationship $BOD_5 + L_5 = L_0$.

$$205.02 + 94.98 = 300 \, \text{mg/L} \quad \checkmark$$

#### Example 3.3: Estimating TDS from EC

**Given:** EC = 1500 µS/cm
**Find:** Approximate TDS

**Step 1:** Apply the conversion formula $TDS \approx 0.55 \times EC$.

$$TDS = 0.55 \times 1500 = 825 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 2:** Compare with the standard. TDS = 825 mg/L exceeds the desirable limit of 500 mg/L but is below the maximum permissible limit of 2000 mg/L.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Relationship Among Key Parameters

| Process | pH | DO | BOD | COD | Turbidity | Microbes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Domestic Sewage | 6.5–7.5 | Low (0–2) | High (200–400) | High (400–800) | High | High |
| Industrial Effluent | Variable (4–10) | Variable | Variable | High | High | Variable |
| Surface Water (Clean) | 7–8.5 | High (8–10) | Low (<3) | Low (<10) | Low | Low |
| Groundwater | 7–8 | Moderate | Very Low | Low | Very Low | Low |
| Treated Wastewater | 6.5–7.5 | ≥5 | <10 | <50 | <10 | <1000 MPN |

### 3.5 Sequence of BOD Progress in a Receiving Stream

1. **Zone of Degradation:** DO decreases, BOD high, fish start dying.
2. **Zone of Active Decomposition:** Septic conditions, anaerobic, foul odors.
3. **Zone of Recovery:** DO rises, BOD drops, algae reappear.
4. **Zone of Cleaner Water:** Full recovery, normal aquatic life.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define BOD and COD. State two differences between them.**

**Model Answer:**

**Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD):** BOD is the amount of dissolved oxygen required by aerobic microorganisms to decompose the biodegradable organic matter in a water sample, under specified conditions of temperature and time (usually 20 °C for 5 days). It measures only the **biodegradable** fraction of organics.

**Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD):** COD is the amount of oxygen required to chemically oxidize both biodegradable and non-biodegradable organic (and certain inorganic) matter in water using a strong chemical oxidant (typically potassium dichromate in acidic medium), measured in 2–3 hours.

| Aspect | BOD | COD |
|---|---|---|
| Oxidation type | Biochemical (microbial) | Chemical (K₂Cr₂O₇) |
| Test duration | 5 days | 2–3 hours |
| Detects | Biodegradable organics only | Total organics (bio + non-bio) |
| Value (typical sewage) | 200–400 mg/L | 400–800 mg/L |

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any six physical characteristics of water with their acceptable limits as per IS 10500.**

**Model Answer:**

The six physical characteristics of water with their permissible limits (IS 10500:2012) are:

1. **Turbidity:** ≤ 1 NTU (max permissible 5 NTU). Measured by Nephelometer.
2. **Color:** ≤ 5 Hazen Units (max 15 Hazen). Measured by Pt-Co colorimeter.
3. **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS):** ≤ 500 mg/L (max 2000 mg/L). Measured gravimetrically.
4. **Total Suspended Solids (TSS):** No specific limit; indicates filtration efficiency.
5. **Odour:** Unobjectionable (qualitative).
6. **Temperature:** Should be palatable; ideally below 25 °C (no formal numerical limit in drinking water standard).

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the physical characteristics of water. Discuss their significance in determining the suitability of water for various uses.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A water sample has calcium hardness of 120 mg/L (as CaCO₃) and magnesium hardness of 60 mg/L (as CaCO₃). Calculate the total hardness and classify the water. If the total alkalinity is 100 mg/L as CaCO₃, comment on the carbonate / non-carbonate hardness.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Physical Characteristics of Water:**

Physical characteristics are those that can be observed by human senses or measured by simple physical instruments without changing the chemical nature of water. They are crucial for determining the aesthetic, operational, and economic viability of water for various uses.

1. **Turbidity:** It is the cloudiness of water caused by suspended particles like clay, silt, organic matter, and microorganisms. Measured in Nephelometric Turbidity Units (NTU) using a Nephelometer based on light scattering. High turbidity provides shelter to pathogens, increases chlorine demand, and interferes with disinfection. IS 10500 prescribes ≤ 1 NTU (desirable), 5 NTU (max). Significance: Drinking water treatment (coagulation-flocculation-sedimentation-filtration) is designed based on influent turbidity.

2. **Color:** True color arises from dissolved organic substances (tannins, humic acids), while apparent color includes suspended matter. Measured in Hazen Units using a Platinum-Cobalt colorimeter. Standard: ≤ 5 HU. Significance: Indicates the presence of decaying vegetation, industrial dyes, or metals like iron and manganese. Color in industrial water affects product appearance in textiles, paper, and food industries.

3. **Odor and Taste:** Caused by dissolved gases (H₂S, CH₄), organic compounds, or chlorination by-products. Measured using the Threshold Odor Number (TON) — the dilution factor at which odor becomes just detectable. Significance: Affects consumer acceptability; presence of certain odors may indicate industrial pollution (phenol odor), sewage (H₂S), or decomposition.

4. **Temperature:** Affects density, viscosity, gas solubility, and biological activity. Thermal pollution from power plants and steel industries reduces DO saturation and disrupts aquatic life. Significance: For drinking water, temperature affects palatability; for aquatic ecosystems, it determines species survival.

5. **Total Suspended Solids (TSS):** Filterable residue retained on a 1.5 µm glass-fiber filter, dried at 103–105 °C, expressed in mg/L. Significance: High TSS reduces light penetration in lakes (affects photosynthesis), deposits sludge in reservoirs, and increases wear in pumps and pipes.

6. **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS):** Solids that pass through the filter, obtained by evaporating and drying the filtrate at 180 °C, expressed in mg/L. Significance: High TDS causes taste problems, scale formation in boilers, and corrosion. IS 10500 limit: 500 mg/L (desirable), 2000 mg/L (max).

7. **Electrical Conductivity (EC):** Ability of water to conduct current due to dissolved ions. Measured in µS/cm. Significance: Indicates salinity; TDS ≈ 0.55 × EC. Used to monitor groundwater intrusion and irrigation suitability.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** Calcium Hardness (CH) = 120 mg/L as CaCO₃; Magnesium Hardness (MH) = 60 mg/L as CaCO₃; Total Alkalinity = 100 mg/L as CaCO₃.

**Step 1:** Calculate Total Hardness.

$$TH = CH + MH = 120 + 60 = 180 \, \text{mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 2:** Classify the water. TH = 180 mg/L falls in the range 121–180 mg/L, hence it is classified as **Hard water**.

**Step 3:** Compare TH with Total Alkalinity. Here TH (180) > Alkalinity (100).

**Step 4:** Determine Carbonate and Non-Carbonate Hardness.

- Since TH > Alkalinity, **Carbonate Hardness (CH) = Alkalinity = 100 mg/L as CaCO₃**.
- **Non-Carbonate Hardness (NCH) = TH − Alkalinity = 180 − 100 = 80 mg/L as CaCO₃**.

**Step 5:** Comment: Since carbonate hardness < total hardness, the water contains both carbonate and non-carbonate hardness. The non-carbonate hardness (80 mg/L) is due to chlorides, sulfates, and nitrates of Ca and Mg, and cannot be removed by simple boiling — it requires **lime-soda softening** or **ion exchange**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define DO, BOD, and COD. Describe the Winkler method for the determination of Dissolved Oxygen. List the factors affecting DO of natural waters.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wastewater sample has an ultimate BOD ($L_0$) of 400 mg/L. If the deoxygenation rate constant k = 0.20 per day, calculate (i) the 5-day BOD, (ii) the 7-day BOD, and (iii) the BOD remaining after 10 days.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — DO, BOD, COD Definitions and Winkler Method:**

**Dissolved Oxygen (DO):** The amount of gaseous oxygen (O₂) physically dissolved in water, expressed in mg/L. It is essential for the respiration of aquatic organisms and for the aerobic decomposition of organic matter. Surface waters normally contain 8–10 mg/L at 20 °C.

**Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD):** The quantity of dissolved oxygen required by aerobic microorganisms to oxidize the biodegradable organic matter in water under standard conditions (20 °C, 5 days). Unit: mg/L.

**Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD):** The quantity of oxygen consumed in the chemical oxidation of organic matter using a strong oxidant (K₂Cr₂O₇) in acidic medium. Unit: mg/L.

**Winkler Method (Modified Azide Modification) — Procedure:**

1. **Fixation:** The BOD bottle is filled completely with the sample (no air bubbles). Add 1 mL each of **manganous sulfate (MnSO₄)** and **alkali-iodide-azide (NaOH + KI + NaN₃)** reagents. A brownish-white precipitate of **manganese hydroxide (Mn(OH)₂)** forms, which in the presence of DO gets oxidized to **manganese hydroxide (MnO(OH)₂)**.

2. **Acidification:** Add 1 mL concentrated H₂SO₄. The precipitate dissolves, liberating free iodine (I₂) equivalent to the DO originally present.

3. **Titration:** Titrate the liberated I₂ with standard **sodium thiosulfate (Na₂S₂O₃)** solution using starch indicator. The blue color disappears at the endpoint.

4. **Calculation:**

$$DO \, (\text{mg/L}) = \frac{V \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V_s}$$

Where V = volume of thiosulfate, N = normality of thiosulfate, 8 = equivalent weight of O₂, V_s = sample volume.

**Factors Affecting DO of Natural Waters:**

1. **Temperature:** Higher temperature → lower DO saturation.
2. **Atmospheric pressure:** Lower pressure (high altitude) → lower DO.
3. **Salinity:** Higher salinity → lower DO.
4. **Photosynthesis:** Aquatic plants release O₂ during daytime, increasing DO.
5. **Respiration and Decomposition:** Microbial activity consumes DO.
6. **Aeration:** Wave action, waterfalls, and turbulence increase DO.
7. **BOD load:** High organic pollution depletes DO rapidly.
8. **Flow velocity:** Stagnant waters have lower DO than flowing ones.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $L_0 = 400 \, \text{mg/L}$, $k = 0.20 \, \text{day}^{-1}$.

**Step 1:** The governing equation is $BOD_t = L_0(1 - e^{-k \cdot t})$.

**Step 2:** Calculate the 5-day BOD.

$$BOD_5 = 400 \times \left(1 - e^{-0.20 \times 5}\right) = 400 \times \left(1 - e^{-1.0}\right)$$

$$e^{-1.0} = 0.3679$$

$$BOD_5 = 400 \times (1 - 0.3679) = 400 \times 0.6321 = 252.84 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the 7-day BOD.

$$BOD_7 = 400 \times \left(1 - e^{-0.20 \times 7}\right) = 400 \times \left(1 - e^{-1.4}\right)$$

$$e^{-1.4} = 0.2466$$

$$BOD_7 = 400 \times (1 - 0.2466) = 400 \times 0.7534 = 301.36 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the BOD remaining after 10 days.

$$L_{10} = L_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t} = 400 \times e^{-0.20 \times 10} = 400 \times e^{-2.0}$$

$$e^{-2.0} = 0.1353$$

$$L_{10} = 400 \times 0.1353 = 54.13 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 5:** (Optional) Calculate the BOD exerted at day 10 for verification.

$$BOD_{10} = L_0 - L_{10} = 400 - 54.13 = 345.87 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Final Answers:** BOD₅ = **252.84 mg/L**, BOD₇ = **301.36 mg/L**, BOD remaining after 10 days = **54.13 mg/L**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Formula confusion:** Students often write $BOD_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ — this is the **remaining BOD**, not the BOD exerted. The exerted BOD is $L_0(1 - e^{-kt})$. This is the **#1 mark-losing mistake** in BOD problems.
> - **Hardness factors:** Forgetting the 2.5 and 4.1 conversion factors (which convert mg/L of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ to mg/L as CaCO₃) results in zero marks.
> - **Units:** Not mentioning units (mg/L, NTU, Hazen) leads to a half-mark deduction in KTU valuation.
> - **BOD/COD relationship:** Writing $BOD_5 = COD$ (which is incorrect; the ratio is approximately 0.68) costs marks.
> - **Standard values:** Not citing the IS 10500 standard values loses at least 1–2 marks in definition-type questions.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Water quality is a composite of physical, chemical, and biological parameters** — never treat them in isolation.
- **Physical parameters (Turbidity, Color, TDS, TSS, EC, Temperature, Odor)** affect aesthetics, operability, and treatment cost; they are usually tested first.
- **Chemical parameters (pH, Hardness, Alkalinity, DO, BOD, COD, Chlorides, Fluorides, Heavy Metals)** determine the chemical stability and pollution level of water.
- **Biological parameters (Total Coliforms, Fecal Coliforms, MPN, Pathogens)** are direct indicators of fecal pollution and public health risk.
- **BOD equation to memorize:** $\boxed{BOD_t = L_0(1 - e^{-kt})}$ — the most heavily tested formula in KTU exams.
- **Hardness formula to memorize:** $\boxed{TH = 2.5[Ca^{2+}] + 4.1[Mg^{2+}] \, (\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3)}$
- **BOD₅ ≈ 0.68 × COD** is an empirical thumb rule for domestic sewage.
- **DO saturation decreases with temperature and salinity** — this is a high-yield concept.
- **pH range for drinking water: 6.5 to 8.5** (IS 10500:2012).
- **TDS limit: 500 mg/L desirable, 2000 mg/L max permissible.**
- **Total Coliforms: 0 MPN/100 mL** is the ideal drinking water standard.
- **Winkler method** is the classical iodometric titration for DO determination — memorize the four steps (MnSO₄ → Alkali-iodide-azide → H₂SO₄ → Na₂S₂O₃ titration).
- **Fluoride limit: 0.6–1.2 mg/L** is beneficial; > 1.5 mg/L causes fluorosis.
- **Nitrate limit: ≤ 45 mg/L** (causes blue baby syndrome in infants).
- **Receiving stream DO sag curve** has 4 zones: degradation, active decomposition, recovery, and clean water.
- **IS 10500:2012** is the **must-cite** Indian Standard for drinking water; **IS 3025** is for test methods.
- **MPN (Most Probable Number)** is a statistical estimate, not a direct count, of bacterial density.
- **BOD test takes 5 days at 20 °C** — remember the temperature and duration for KTU 2-mark and 3-mark questions.
- **In EIA, baseline water quality data is mandatory** for projects discharging effluent into any water body.

---

> **End of Notes — Module 1: Physical, Chemical and Biological Characteristics of Water | OEBBT617 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Quality Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Water Quality Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Quality Standards (WQS)** are legally enforceable or recommended threshold limits for physical, chemical, biological, and radiological parameters of water, prescribed by regulatory authorities to protect designated uses such as drinking, irrigation, aquaculture, recreation, and industrial cooling.

In KTU 2024 terminology, water quality standards act as the **quantitative regulatory benchmark** against which the baseline environmental quality of a proposed development project is compared during the **Screening, Scoping, and EIA Report preparation** stages. If the predicted post-mitigation concentration exceeds the standard, it constitutes a **significant adverse impact** under the EIA Notification, 2006 (and its 2020 amendments).

The principal Indian standards that govern water quality assessments in EIA studies are:

- **IS 10500:2012** — Indian Standard Drinking Water — Specification (revised by BIS).
- **CPCB Designated Best Use (DBU) Classification** of inland surface waters.
- **WHO Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality (4th Edition, 2017)**.
- **IS 2296:1982 (Reaffirmed 2014)** — Tolerance Limits for Inland Surface Waters.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Hospital Report Card":** Think of water as a patient undergoing a health check-up. A doctor measures sugar (BOD), blood pressure (pH), cholesterol (TDS), and the presence of infection (coliforms). The lab report has a *normal range column* — anything outside it is flagged. Water Quality Standards are precisely those "normal ranges" written into law. They tell engineers whether the water is "healthy enough" for drinking, farming, or fish survival, much like a doctor's reference values tell you whether the patient is healthy.

### 1.3 Classification of Water Quality Parameters

Water quality parameters are conventionally grouped into four families:

| Family | Examples | Measured In | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Physical** | Turbidity, Colour, Odour, TDS, Temperature | NTU, Pt-Co units, mg/L, °C | Aesthetic & thermal suitability |
| **Chemical** | pH, Hardness, Cl⁻, F⁻, NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, Heavy metals | mg/L or pH units | Toxicity, palatability, corrosion |
| **Biological** | Total Coliforms, Faecal Coliforms, E. coli | MPN/100 mL | Pathogenic risk |
| **Radiological** | Gross α, Gross β activity | Bq/L | Long-term cancer risk |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** "**P**hysical **C**hemical **B**iological **R**adiological" → **PCBR** — the four pillars of WQ monitoring used in every EIA chapter.

### 1.4 Why Water Quality Standards Matter in EIA

EIA studies under the **EIA Notification, 2006** require the project proponent to:

1. Establish the **baseline water quality** of receiving water bodies (rivers, lakes, groundwater aquifers).
2. Predict the **post-project effluent quality** using mathematical dispersion models (Streeter–Phelps, QUAL2E).
3. Compare predicted concentrations with **regulatory standards**.
4. Propose **mitigation measures** (ETPs, STPs, dilution zones) if standards are violated.

Without numerical standards, EIA is reduced to a qualitative narrative — standards therefore provide the **objective yardstick** for decision-making.

### 1.5 Standards-Setting Authorities

| Authority | Jurisdiction | Key Document |
|---|---|---|
| **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** | National — Drinking Water | IS 10500:2012 |
| **CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board)** | National — Effluents & Surface Water | Schedule VI, DBU Rules |
| **WHO** | International — Guideline values | GDWQ 4th Edition |
| **EPA (USA)** | International Reference | NPDWS, MCL |
| **ICMR** | India — Bacteriological | Drinking Water Guidelines |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Designated Best Use (DBU) Classification — CPCB

The CPCB classifies Indian inland surface waters into **five classes (A–E)** based on their designated best use. This classification is the cornerstone of Indian surface-water quality regulation.

| Class | Designated Best Use | Critical Parameter Limits |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Drinking Water Source (without conventional treatment but after disinfection) | Total Coliforms ≤ **50 MPN/100 mL**; pH **6.5–8.5**; DO ≥ **6 mg/L**; BOD ≤ **2 mg/L** |
| **B** | Outdoor Bathing (Organised) | Total Coliforms ≤ **500 MPN/100 mL**; pH 6.5–8.5; DO ≥ **5 mg/L**; BOD ≤ **3 mg/L** |
| **C** | Drinking Water Source (with conventional treatment) | Total Coliforms ≤ **5000 MPN/100 mL**; DO ≥ **4 mg/L**; BOD ≤ **3 mg/L** |
| **D** | Propagation of Wildlife, Fisheries | DO ≥ **4 mg/L**; pH 6.5–8.5; Free NH₃ ≤ **1.2 mg/L** |
| **E** | Irrigation, Industrial Cooling, Controlled Waste Disposal | pH 6.0–8.5; EC ≤ **2250 µS/cm**; SAR ≤ **26** |

### 2.2 IS 10500:2012 — Indian Standard Drinking Water Specification

IS 10500:2012 (second revision) specifies two categories of limits:

- **Required (Mandatory) Limits** — Must be complied with under all circumstances.
- **Desirable Limits** — Aim for compliance; relaxation permitted only when no alternative source exists.

### 2.3 Critical Parameter Limits (IS 10500:2012)

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Desirable Limit | Permissible Limit (No Alternative) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | pH | **6.5 – 8.5** | No relaxation |
| 2 | Turbidity (NTU) | **1** | **5** |
| 3 | Total Dissolved Solids (mg/L) | **500** | **2000** |
| 4 | Total Hardness as CaCO₃ (mg/L) | **200** | **600** |
| 5 | Chloride (mg/L) | **250** | **1000** |
| 6 | Fluoride (mg/L) | **1.0** | **1.5** |
| 7 | Nitrate (as NO₃⁻) (mg/L) | **45** | No relaxation |
| 8 | Iron (mg/L) | **0.3** | No relaxation |
| 9 | Sulphate (mg/L) | **200** | **400** |
| 10 | Total Coliforms (MPN/100 mL) | **0** | **0** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students memorise only the *desirable* limits and forget that *permissible limits* apply **only when no alternative source is available** — examiners specifically test this distinction for 3-mark questions.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Water Quality Index (WQI)

The **Water Quality Index** is a single composite number (0–100) that summarises the overall fitness of water for a designated use. It is widely asked in KTU numericals.

**Step 1 — Compute Unit Weight (wᵢ):**

$$w_i = \frac{K}{S_i}, \quad \text{where} \quad K = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{S_i}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Quality Rating (qᵢ):**

$$q_i = 100 \times \frac{V_i - V_{ideal}}{S_i - V_{ideal}}$$

- $V_i$ = observed value of parameter *i*
- $V_{ideal}$ = ideal value (pH = 7; all others = 0)
- $S_i$ = standard limit of parameter *i*

**Step 3 — Compute WQI:**

$$WQI = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot q_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$$

**WQI Interpretation (Brown's Classification):**

| WQI Range | Water Quality Status |
|---|---|
| **0 – 25** | Excellent |
| **26 – 50** | Good |
| **51 – 75** | Poor |
| **76 – 100** | Very Poor |
| **> 100** | Unsuitable for drinking |

### 2.5 Standards for Industrial Effluents (Schedule VI)

Industrial effluents discharged into inland surface waters must conform to **CPCB Schedule VI** limits. Some representative values:

| Industry | pH | BOD (mg/L) | COD (mg/L) | TSS (mg/L) | Oil & Grease (mg/L) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dye & Dye Intermediate** | 6.0–8.5 | 30 | 250 | 100 | 10 |
| **Sugar** | 6.5–8.5 | 30 | 250 | 100 | 10 |
| **Distillery** | 6.5–8.5 | 30 | 400 | 210 | 10 |
| **Pulp & Paper** | 5.5–9.0 | 30 | 350 | 100 | 10 |
| **Tannery** | 6.5–8.5 | 30 | 250 | 100 | 10 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** An EIA consultant for a proposed sugar mill in Kerala must design an Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP) capable of delivering BOD ≤ 30 mg/L. Anything higher means statutory non-compliance and rejection of Environmental Clearance (EC) by the State Expert Appraisal Committee (SEAC).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Water Quality Index (WQI) from First Principles

The WQI is a *weighted arithmetic mean* of normalised sub-indices. The derivation proceeds in three rigorous stages.

#### Stage 1 — Selection of Parameters and Standards

For a drinking-water study, we choose *n* = 6 parameters: pH, TDS, Total Hardness, Chloride, Fluoride, and Nitrate. The BIS desirable limits ($S_i$) for these are:

$$
S_{pH} = 8.5, \quad S_{TDS} = 500, \quad S_{TH} = 200, \quad S_{Cl} = 250, \quad S_{F} = 1.0, \quad S_{NO_3} = 45
$$

#### Stage 2 — Derivation of the Proportionality Constant K

The unit weight $w_i$ must be **inversely proportional** to the standard $S_i$, so that strict parameters (low $S_i$, e.g., fluoride) carry more weight:

$$
w_i \propto \frac{1}{S_i} \quad \Rightarrow \quad w_i = \frac{K}{S_i}
$$

To normalise, we enforce:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{K}{S_i} = 1
$$

Solving for the proportionality constant:

$$
K = \frac{1}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{S_i}}
$$

#### Stage 3 — Derivation of Quality Rating qᵢ

A quality rating of **0** should correspond to the ideal water (all pollutants = 0, pH = 7), and **100** should correspond to the threshold $S_i$. A linear interpolation gives:

$$
\frac{q_i - 0}{100 - 0} = \frac{V_i - V_{ideal}}{S_i - V_{ideal}}
$$

Solving for $q_i$:

$$
q_i = 100 \cdot \frac{V_i - V_{ideal}}{S_i - V_{ideal}}
$$

#### Stage 4 — Final WQI

The composite index is the weighted average:

$$
\boxed{WQI = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot q_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A groundwater sample from a site proposed for a residential township yields the following observations:

| Parameter | Observed ($V_i$) | Standard ($S_i$) | Ideal ($V_{ideal}$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| pH | 7.8 | 8.5 | 7.0 |
| TDS (mg/L) | 620 | 500 | 0 |
| Total Hardness (mg/L) | 280 | 200 | 0 |
| Chloride (mg/L) | 310 | 250 | 0 |
| Fluoride (mg/L) | 1.4 | 1.0 | 0 |
| Nitrate (mg/L) | 52 | 45 | 0 |

**Compute the WQI and comment on fitness for drinking.**

**Step A — Compute $K$:**

$$
\sum \frac{1}{S_i} = \frac{1}{8.5} + \frac{1}{500} + \frac{1}{200} + \frac{1}{250} + \frac{1}{1.0} + \frac{1}{45}
$$

$$
\sum \frac{1}{S_i} = 0.1176 + 0.0020 + 0.0050 + 0.0040 + 1.0000 + 0.0222
$$

$$
\sum \frac{1}{S_i} = 1.1508
$$

$$
K = \frac{1}{1.1508} = 0.8689
$$

**Step B — Compute $w_i$ and $q_i$ for each parameter:**

$$
w_{pH} = \frac{0.8689}{8.5} = 0.1022, \quad q_{pH} = 100 \cdot \frac{7.8 - 7.0}{8.5 - 7.0} = 53.33
$$

$$
w_{TDS} = \frac{0.8689}{500} = 0.00174, \quad q_{TDS} = 100 \cdot \frac{620 - 0}{500 - 0} = 124.0
$$

$$
w_{TH} = \frac{0.8689}{200} = 0.00434, \quad q_{TH} = 100 \cdot \frac{280 - 0}{200 - 0} = 140.0
$$

$$
w_{Cl} = \frac{0.8689}{250} = 0.00348, \quad q_{Cl} = 100 \cdot \frac{310 - 0}{250 - 0} = 124.0
$$

$$
w_{F} = \frac{0.8689}{1.0} = 0.8689, \quad q_{F} = 100 \cdot \frac{1.4 - 0}{1.0 - 0} = 140.0
$$

$$
w_{NO_3} = \frac{0.8689}{45} = 0.01931, \quad q_{NO_3} = 100 \cdot \frac{52 - 0}{45 - 0} = 115.56
$$

**Step C — Tabulate and Compute WQI:**

| Parameter | $w_i$ | $q_i$ | $w_i \times q_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| pH | 0.1022 | 53.33 | 5.45 |
| TDS | 0.00174 | 124.00 | 0.22 |
| Total Hardness | 0.00434 | 140.00 | 0.61 |
| Chloride | 0.00348 | 124.00 | 0.43 |
| Fluoride | 0.86890 | 140.00 | 121.65 |
| Nitrate | 0.01931 | 115.56 | 2.23 |
| **Sum** | **0.99999 ≈ 1.0** | — | **130.59** |

$$
WQI = \frac{130.59}{1.0} = 130.59
$$

**Interpretation:** WQI = 130.59 lies in the **> 100** range → **Unsuitable for drinking** without treatment. The township EIA report must therefore propose a **Defluoridation Unit** and **RO Plant** before commissioning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice how **fluoride dominates** the WQI because its unit weight $w_F = 0.869$ is nearly **86 %** of the total — this correctly reflects the strict permissible limit of **1.0 mg/L** for fluoride in IS 10500.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Water Quality Standards

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the regulatory hierarchy of WQS as applied in EIA studies in India.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Water Quality Standards] --> B[Drinking Water]
    A --> C[Surface Water]
    A --> D[Industrial Effluent]
    A --> E[Groundwater]

    B --> B1[IS 10500:2012]
    B --> B2[WHO GDWQ 4th Ed 2017]
    B --> B3[EPA NPDWS MCL]

    C --> C1[CPCB DBU Class A to E]
    C --> C2[IS 2296:1982]
    C --> C3[Schedule VI Effluent Limits]

    D --> D1[CPCB Schedule VI]
    D --> D2[Sector Specific Limits]
    D --> D3[SPCB Stricter Norms]

    E --> E1[IS 10500:2012]
    E --> E2[CGWB Guidelines]
    E --> E3[State PCB Notification]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style B fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#ea580c,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff
```


### 4.3 CPCB Designated Best Use Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Surface Water Sample] --> T{Total Coliforms MPN per 100 mL}
    T -- lt 50 --> A[Class A Drinking without treatment]
    T -- 50 to 500 --> B1{DO mg per L}
    T -- 500 to 5000 --> C[Class C Drinking with conventional treatment]
    T -- gt 5000 --> D1{pH and Use}
    D1 -- pH 6.5 to 8.5 Wildlife --> D[Class D Fisheries Wildlife]
    D1 -- pH 6.0 to 8.5 EC lt 2250 --> E[Class E Irrigation Industrial]

    B1 -- DO ge 5 --> B[Class B Bathing Organised]

    style S fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff

```

### 4.4 WQI Computation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Parameter Observations V sub i] --> Q[Stage 1 Compute K]
    Q --> R[Stage 2 Compute w sub i and q sub i]
    R --> S[Stage 3 Weighted Sum]
    S --> T[Stage 4 Normalise by Sigma w sub i]
    T --> U[WQI Value 0 to 100 plus]
    U --> V{Interpretation}
    V -- lt 25 --> W1[Excellent]
    V -- 26 to 50 --> W2[Good]
    V -- 51 to 75 --> W3[Poor]
    V -- 76 to 100 --> W4[Very Poor]
    V -- gt 100 --> W5[Unsuitable]

    style U fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style V fill:#facc15,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **desirable limits** and **permissible limits** as per IS 10500:2012. Why are permissible limits not preferred even though they are legally enforceable?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Desirable limits** (e.g., TDS = 500 mg/L, Fluoride = 1.0 mg/L) are the *ideal* concentrations that pose no health risk over a lifetime of consumption (1 mark).
- **Permissible limits** (e.g., TDS = 2000 mg/L, Fluoride = 1.5 mg/L) are the *maximum* concentrations allowed only when no alternative water source is available (1 mark).
- **Why not preferred:** Permissible limits indicate the *onset of detectable health impact* — long-term consumption at permissible fluoride (1.5 mg/L) causes skeletal fluorosis; long-term high TDS causes renal stress and taste aversion (1 mark).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** List any **six Designated Best Use classes** of inland surface waters as per CPCB and state the critical parameter that determines each class.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. Class A — Drinking Water (without conventional treatment) → Total Coliforms ≤ 50 MPN/100 mL (0.5 mark).
2. Class B — Outdoor Bathing → Total Coliforms ≤ 500 MPN/100 mL (0.5 mark).
3. Class C — Drinking Water (with conventional treatment) → Total Coliforms ≤ 5000 MPN/100 mL (0.5 mark).
4. Class D — Propagation of Wildlife & Fisheries → Free Ammonia ≤ 1.2 mg/L (0.5 mark).
5. Class E — Irrigation & Industrial Cooling → EC ≤ 2250 µS/cm and SAR ≤ 26 (1 mark).

*(Any six with correct critical parameter — full 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **Module I — Internal Choice**
> **Answer any ONE full question from the following.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **IS 10500:2012** specification for drinking water with reference to its scope, parameter categories, and significance in EIA studies. List the permissible limits for **pH, TDS, Total Hardness, Fluoride, and Nitrate**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A river receiving treated effluent from a proposed paper mill has the following baseline characteristics: DO = 7.5 mg/L, BOD = 2 mg/L, Total Coliforms = 200 MPN/100 mL. Identify the **CPCB Designated Best Use class** and justify with reasoning. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *(7 marks breakdown)*

**Scope of IS 10500:2012:** It prescribes the quality parameters and their limits for drinking water supplied through *piped* distribution networks as well as *packaged drinking water*. It applies to both **treated** and **untreated** sources intended for human consumption (2 marks).

**Parameter Categories:** Four families — Physical (Turbidity, Colour, Odour, TDS, Temperature), Chemical (pH, Hardness, Cl⁻, F⁻, NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, Fe, Mn, Heavy Metals, Pesticides), Bacteriological (Total Coliforms, Faecal Coliforms, E. coli), and Radiological (Gross α & β activity) (2 marks).

**Permissible Limits Table:**

| Parameter | Permissible Limit |
|---|---|
| pH | 6.5 – 8.5 (no relaxation) |
| TDS | 2000 mg/L |
| Total Hardness | 600 mg/L |
| Fluoride | 1.5 mg/L |
| Nitrate | 45 mg/L (no relaxation) |

(2 marks)

**Significance in EIA:** Acts as the compliance benchmark for baseline and post-project water quality of freshwater sources around the project site (1 mark).

**(b)** *(7 marks breakdown)*

Given: DO = 7.5 mg/L, BOD = 2 mg/L, Total Coliforms = 200 MPN/100 mL.

**Comparison with CPCB DBU criteria:**

| Class | Coliforms | DO | BOD | Match? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | ≤ 50 MPN | ≥ 6 | ≤ 2 | **Failed** — Coliforms = 200 > 50 |
| B | ≤ 500 MPN | ≥ 5 | ≤ 3 | **Passed** (1 mark) |
| C | ≤ 5000 MPN | ≥ 4 | ≤ 3 | **Passed** but uses lower-quality source |

The river best fits **Class B — Outdoor Bathing (Organised)** (2 marks).

**Justification:** Total Coliforms = 200 MPN (within 50–500 range, indicating moderate faecal contamination); DO = 7.5 mg/L is healthy (> 5 mg/L); BOD = 2 mg/L is low, indicating minimal organic pollution (3 marks). The river *cannot* be used as Class A drinking water without treatment because coliform levels exceed 50 MPN. Best suited for organised outdoor bathing under Class B.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):** Many students identify the river as "Class A" because DO is high, missing the coliform criterion. **Always check ALL three parameters (Coliforms + DO + BOD) before finalising the class.** Losing the coliform check typically costs **3 of the 7 marks**.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)**

**(a)** Define **Water Quality Index (WQI)**. Derive the equations for unit weight ($w_i$) and quality rating ($q_i$). Explain the significance of the proportionality constant $K$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a groundwater sample, the following values were observed: pH = 7.4, TDS = 480 mg/L, Total Hardness = 180 mg/L, Chloride = 200 mg/L, Fluoride = 1.1 mg/L. Compute the **WQI** using IS 10500:2012 desirable limits and comment on its suitability for drinking. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *(7 marks breakdown)*

**Definition:** The WQI is a single composite number (0–100+) representing the aggregate impact of multiple water quality parameters on the overall fitness of water for a designated use (1 mark).

**Derivation of Unit Weight $w_i$:**

Since parameters with stricter (lower) standards should carry greater weight, the unit weight is taken inversely proportional to the standard limit:

$$
w_i = \frac{K}{S_i} \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

Applying the normalisation condition $\sum w_i = 1$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{K}{S_i} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = \frac{1}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{S_i}} \quad (3 \text{ marks})
$$

**Derivation of Quality Rating $q_i$:**

A linear interpolation between ideal ($V_{ideal}$, gives $q_i = 0$) and standard ($S_i$, gives $q_i = 100$):

$$
q_i = 100 \cdot \frac{V_i - V_{ideal}}{S_i - V_{ideal}} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Significance of $K$:** $K$ ensures that the sum of all unit weights equals unity, thereby converting *raw* inverse-standard weights into **dimensionless, normalised** weights that can be combined (1 mark).

**(b)** *(7 marks breakdown)*

**Step 1 — Set Standards from IS 10500:2012 desirable limits:**

$$
S_{pH} = 8.5, \quad S_{TDS} = 500, \quad S_{TH} = 200, \quad S_{Cl} = 250, \quad S_{F} = 1.0
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $K$:**

$$
\sum \frac{1}{S_i} = \frac{1}{8.5} + \frac{1}{500} + \frac{1}{200} + \frac{1}{250} + \frac{1}{1.0}
$$

$$
\sum \frac{1}{S_i} = 0.1176 + 0.0020 + 0.0050 + 0.0040 + 1.0000 = 1.1286
$$

$$
K = \frac{1}{1.1286} = 0.8861 \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $w_i$ and $q_i$:**

| Parameter | $w_i = K/S_i$ | $q_i = 100 \cdot (V_i - V_{ideal}) / (S_i - V_{ideal})$ |
|---|---|---|
| pH | 0.1043 | $100 \times (7.4-7.0)/(8.5-7.0) = 26.67$ |
| TDS | 0.00177 | $100 \times 480/500 = 96.00$ |
| TH | 0.00443 | $100 \times 180/200 = 90.00$ |
| Cl | 0.00354 | $100 \times 200/250 = 80.00$ |
| F | 0.88610 | $100 \times 1.1/1.0 = 110.00$ |

(3 marks for tabulation)

**Step 4 — Compute WQI:**

$$
\sum w_i q_i = (0.1043 \times 26.67) + (0.00177 \times 96.00) + (0.00443 \times 90.00) + (0.00354 \times 80.00) + (0.88610 \times 110.00)
$$

$$
\sum w_i q_i = 2.781 + 0.170 + 0.399 + 0.283 + 97.471 = 101.10 \quad (2 \text{ marks})
$$

$$
\sum w_i = 0.1043 + 0.00177 + 0.00443 + 0.00354 + 0.88610 = 1.00014 \approx 1.0
$$

$$
\boxed{WQI = \frac{101.10}{1.0} = 101.10}
$$

**Interpretation:** WQI = 101.10 lies in the **> 100** band → water is **unsuitable for drinking** without treatment (1 mark). The dominant contributor is **fluoride** (weight 0.886), whose observed value 1.1 mg/L exceeds the desirable limit 1.0 mg/L. Recommended mitigation: **Defluoridation** via activated alumina or RO treatment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B — Question B):** Two common errors that cost marks:
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sum w_i$ denominator** — students often directly write $WQI = \sum w_i q_i$ without normalising. Always divide by $\sum w_i$ (even if it equals ~1.0, show it explicitly).
> 2. **Setting $V_{ideal} = 0$ for pH** — pH has an ideal value of **7.0**, not 0. This single mistake can shift the result by ~5–10 %.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Water Quality Standards**

- ⭐ **IS 10500:2012** is the *primary* Indian standard for drinking water; remember both **desirable** and **permissible** limits.
- ⭐ **CPCB DBU** classifies inland surface waters into **5 classes (A to E)** based on **Total Coliforms, DO, BOD, EC, and SAR**.
- ⭐ **Fluoride** has the **strictest** desirable limit (1.0 mg/L) and dominates WQI computations due to its high unit weight.
- ⭐ **pH ideal** for WQI computation = **7.0** (not 0).
- ⭐ **Nitrate** has **no permissible relaxation** — its limit is 45 mg/L under all circumstances.
- ⭐ **Schedule VI** of the Environment (Protection) Rules, 1986 governs **industrial effluent** discharge limits.
- ⭐ WQI > 100 ⇒ **Unsuitable for drinking**; 0–25 ⇒ Excellent; 26–50 ⇒ Good; 51–75 ⇒ Poor; 76–100 ⇒ Very Poor.
- ⭐ **Permissible limits** are legal only when **no alternative source exists** — examiners test this distinction every year.
- ⭐ In EIA studies, predicted post-project water quality is **always compared** with the relevant standard; violation = **significant impact** requiring mitigation.
- ⭐ **Total Coliforms in drinking water = 0 MPN/100 mL** under both desirable and permissible limits — there is **no relaxation** for bacteriological quality.
- ⭐ **DO** is the master indicator of river health: ≥ 6 mg/L (Class A), ≥ 5 mg/L (Class B), ≥ 4 mg/L (Class C & D).
- ⭐ WHO guideline values are **advisory**, while BIS/CPCB values are **statutorily enforceable** — know the difference.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Pollution: Primary and Secondary Pollutants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Air Pollution: Primary and Secondary Pollutants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Air Pollution — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Pollution** is defined as the presence of one or more contaminants (gaseous, liquid, or solid) in the outdoor atmosphere in quantities, characteristics, and duration that are injurious to human health, animal and plant life, property, or that unreasonably interfere with the comfortable enjoyment of life or the conduct of business.

The **U.S. Clean Air Act (1970)** and the **Indian Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** both treat air pollution as a statutory offence when ambient concentrations of any pollutant exceed prescribed **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** or **WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines (2021)**.

**Standard Metrics Used in EIA Reports:**

- **µg/m³** — micrograms per cubic metre (used for particulates and trace gases)
- **ppb / ppm** — parts per billion / parts per million (used for gaseous pollutants)
- **AQI** — Air Quality Index (composite numerical scale 0–500)
- **8-hour / 24-hour / annual averages** — time-weighted averaging periods

### 1.2 Primary Pollutants — The Direct Emitters

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Pollutants** are those contaminants that are emitted directly into the atmosphere from an identifiable source in a form that is already harmful to human health, vegetation, or materials. They undergo no chemical transformation in the atmosphere prior to their first impact.

**Principal Primary Pollutants (KTU 2024 Module 1 Syllabus):**

- **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** — from incomplete combustion of fossil fuels
- **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)** — from combustion of sulphur-bearing fuels
- **Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ = NO + NO₂)** — from high-temperature combustion
- **Particulate Matter (PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅)** — from industrial and vehicular sources
- **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)** — from solvents, fuels, paints
- **Lead (Pb)** — from leaded gasoline and smelters
- **Ammonia (NH₃)** — from agricultural and industrial activity

### 1.3 Secondary Pollutants — The Atmospheric Derivatives

> [!NOTE]
> **Secondary Pollutants** are those pollutants that are *not emitted directly* but form in the atmosphere through chemical reactions between primary pollutants and normal atmospheric constituents (such as water vapour, oxygen, and sunlight).

**Principal Secondary Pollutants:**

- **Ground-level Ozone (O₃)** — photochemical reaction of NOₓ + VOCs + sunlight
- **Sulphuric Acid (H₂SO₄)** — oxidation of SO₂ to form acid rain
- **Nitric Acid (HNO₃)** — oxidation of NO₂ forming acid deposition
- **Peroxyacetyl Nitrate (PAN)** — photochemical smog component
- **Secondary Particulate Matter (PM)** — sulphate and nitrate aerosols
- **Formaldehyde (HCHO)** and other **aldehydes** — from VOC oxidation

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Chemistry" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Think of the Atmosphere as a Giant Kitchen.**
>
> - **Primary Pollutants = Raw Ingredients** thrown directly into the kitchen from the chimney (factory, vehicle, power plant). They are already "spicy" and harmful the moment they enter.
> - **Atmospheric Reactions = The Stove.** The sun acts as the burner, water vapour as the pot, and oxygen as the helper.
> - **Secondary Pollutants = The Cooked Dish.** The same raw ingredients, when heated, react with each other to form entirely new, often more dangerous chemical entities (e.g., tasty but toxic dish = ozone in smog).

Just as a chef cannot reverse the cooking process easily, secondary pollutants once formed in the troposphere cannot be "un-cooked" without complex photochemistry — and that is exactly why EIA must evaluate both emitted and atmospheric products.

### 1.5 Why Distinguish Primary from Secondary in EIA?

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In EIA studies, merely listing emissions from stacks (primary pollutants) is **insufficient**. The TOR (Terms of Reference) issued by the **SEIAA / Ministry of Environment** explicitly requires the proponent to model the *formation* of secondary pollutants, especially **PM₂.₅, O₃, and acid deposition**, because:
> 1. The damage zone may extend **hundreds of kilometres** downwind (long-range transboundary pollution).
> 2. Receptor ecosystems (forests, lakes, monuments) may suffer *more* from secondary than from primary pollutants.
> 3. Climate-forcing potential of O₃ and secondary aerosols must be quantified for net GHG impact.

## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Primary Pollutant Generation

The generation of primary pollutants follows identifiable stoichiometric and thermodynamic pathways tied to specific human activities.

**Step 1 — Combustion Inefficiency (CO Formation):**

$$ \text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} \quad (\text{incomplete combustion}) $$

When the air–fuel ratio is sub-stoichiometric, carbon is only partially oxidised, releasing **CO** instead of **CO₂**.

**Step 2 — Sulphur in Fuel (SO₂ Formation):**

$$ \text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 $$

Coal and petroleum contain **0.5 % – 5 % sulphur by mass**. Each kilogram of sulphur produces **2.0 kg of SO₂**.

**Step 3 — Thermal NOₓ (Zeldovich Mechanism):**

$$ \text{N}_2 + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{T > 1300^{\circ}\text{C}} 2\text{NO} $$

At flame temperatures exceeding **1300 °C** (typical of internal combustion engines, blast furnaces, gas turbines), atmospheric N₂ dissociates and re-forms as **NO**, which subsequently oxidises to **NO₂**.

**Step 4 — Particulate Emission (PM₁₀ / PM₂.₅):**

- **Mechanical generation**: crushing, grinding, road dust, fly ash.
- **Condensation generation**: vapour-phase metals and unburnt hydrocarbons condensing to fine particles.

**Step 5 — VOCs (Volatile Organic Compounds):**

Released from petroleum handling, solvent evaporation, biogenic emissions (isoprene from trees), and incomplete fuel combustion.

### 2.2 Operational Logic of Secondary Pollutant Generation

Secondary pollutants are *engineered by the atmosphere itself*. The four most important mechanisms are:

**A. Photochemical Smog Formation (Tropospheric O₃ & PAN):**

Initiation step (NO₂ photolysis):

$$ \text{NO}_2 + h\nu \; (\lambda \le 430\,\text{nm}) \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}\cdot $$

Atomic oxygen + molecular oxygen:

$$ \text{O}\cdot + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M} $$

(where M is a third body such as N₂ or O₂ that absorbs excess energy)

Ozone reacts with NO, but VOCs regenerate NO₂, so ozone accumulates:

$$ \text{O}_3 + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2 $$

Hydrocarbon radical attack produces PAN:

$$ \text{CH}_3\text{CO}\cdot + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOO}\cdot $$
$$ \text{CH}_3\text{COOO}\cdot + \text{NO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOONO}_2 \; (\text{PAN}) $$

**B. Acid Rain Formation (H₂SO₄, HNO₃):**

Sulphur dioxide oxidation (homogeneous gas phase):

$$ 2\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{catalyst}} 2\text{SO}_3 $$
$$ \text{SO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 $$

Heterogeneous aqueous phase (in cloud droplets):

$$ \text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_3 $$
$$ 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_3 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 $$

Nitrogen dioxide pathway:

$$ 4\text{NO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\text{HNO}_3 $$

**C. Secondary Inorganic Aerosols (Sulphate & Nitrate PM):**

$$ \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \;(\text{g}) \rightarrow \text{SO}_4^{2-} \;(\text{PM}_{2.5}) $$
$$ \text{HNO}_3 \;(\text{g}) + \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3 \;(\text{PM}_{2.5}) $$

**D. Secondary Organic Aerosols (SOA):**

Oxidation products of VOCs (e.g., pinene, toluene) condense into low-volatility compounds, forming **SOA**, which contributes up to **50 %–80 % of organic PM₂.₅** in urban air.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table — it covers 80 % of numerical questions asked in KTU Module 1.**

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fuel sulphur to SO₂ | $\text{SO}_2 = 2.0 \times S \times M_f$ | kg SO₂ | Emission inventory (S in kg/kg fuel) |
| Mass of air for combustion | $\text{Air} = 11.6 \times C + 34.8 \times (H - O/8) + 4.35 \times S$ | kg/kg fuel | Stoichiometric calculation |
| CO/CO₂ ratio (incomplete combustion) | $\eta = (C - C_{\text{ox}})/C$ | dimensionless | Combustion efficiency |
| Conversion of NO to NO₂ | Photostationary state: $J = k_1 [O_3][NO] / [NO_2]$ | s⁻¹ | Smog modelling |
| Acid rain pH | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10} [H^+]$ | dimensionless | Impact assessment |
| Normalised H⁺ from SO₂ | $[H^+] = 2 \times [\text{SO}_2]_{\text{oxidised}} \times 10^{-6}$ | mol/L | Acid deposition analysis |
| AQI sub-index | $I_p = \dfrac{I_{Hi}-I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi}-BP_{Lo}}(C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$ | dimensionless | EIA reporting |
| Conversion factor ppb ↔ µg/m³ | $\mu g/m^3 = ppb \times \dfrac{M}{24.45}$ | µg/m³ | Unit conversion in EIA tables |
| Particulate emission factor (vehicular) | $E = a \cdot V^b \; (\text{g/km})$ | g/km | Line source estimation |
| Plume rise (Briggs) | $\Delta h = 1.6 F^{1/3} x^{2/3} / u$ | m | Stack height design |

> **Where:** $M$ = molecular weight of gas, $24.45$ = molar volume at **25 °C, 1 atm**; $J$ = photolysis rate; $F$ = buoyancy flux; $u$ = wind speed; $V$ = vehicle speed.

### 2.4 Real-World EIA Utility

- **Industry EIA**: Power plants, cement kilns, and refineries use the SO₂-to-H₂SO₄ conversion to assess downwind acid deposition on forests and monuments (e.g., **Taj Trapezium Case**).
- **Transport EIA**: Highway and airport projects use line-source emission factors to estimate CO, NOₓ, and secondary PM₂.₅.
- **Land-use EIA**: Urban master plans use VOC/NOₓ ratios to predict ground-level O₃ exceedances.
- **Climate EIA**: Secondary aerosols and O₃ are short-lived climate forcers (SLCFs) included in net climate impact.

## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric SO₂ Emission from a Coal-Fired Plant

**Given:**
- Coal consumption rate $\dot{m}_c = 100$ tonnes/hour
- Sulphur content $S\% = 0.8 \%$ by mass
- Conversion efficiency of S to SO₂: $\eta_S = 95\%$

**Step 1 — Mass of sulphur in coal per hour:**

$$ \dot{m}_S = \dot{m}_c \times \frac{S\%}{100} = 100{,}000 \; \text{kg/h} \times 0.008 = 800 \; \text{kg/h} $$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric conversion of S to SO₂:**

The molecular weights are: $M_S = 32$ and $M_{SO_2} = 64$.

$$ \text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 $$

The mass ratio is $64/32 = 2.0$.

**Step 3 — Theoretical SO₂ mass:**

$$ \dot{m}_{SO_2, \text{theoretical}} = \dot{m}_S \times 2.0 = 800 \times 2.0 = 1600 \; \text{kg/h} $$

**Step 4 — Actual SO₂ with conversion efficiency:**

$$ \dot{m}_{SO_2, \text{actual}} = 1600 \times 0.95 = 1520 \; \text{kg/h} $$

**Step 5 — Daily emission:**

$$ 1520 \times 24 = 36{,}480 \; \text{kg/day} \approx 36.48 \; \text{tonnes/day} $$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** This single unit emits 36.5 tonnes of SO₂ per day. If the prevailing wind disperses this over 10 km², daily deposition = 3.65 g/m², which after oxidation to H₂SO₄ contributes significantly to acid rain.

### 3.2 Derivation: Photostationary State of O₃–NO–NO₂

The Leighton Ratio at photostationary equilibrium is:

$$ \phi = \frac{[NO][O_3]}{[NO_2]} = \frac{J_{NO_2}}{k_1} $$

**Step 1 — Write the three key reactions:**

$$ \text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}\cdot \quad (J_{NO_2}) $$
$$ \text{O}\cdot + \text{O}_2 + M \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + M \quad (k_2) $$
$$ \text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \quad (k_1) $$

**Step 2 — Steady-state assumption:**

At photostationary state, formation rate of O₃ = destruction rate of O₃.

$$ \frac{d[O_3]}{dt} = k_2 [O][O_2][M] - k_1 [NO][O_3] = 0 $$

**Step 3 — Solve for [O]:**

$$ [O] = \frac{k_1 [NO][O_3]}{k_2 [O_2][M]} $$

**Step 4 — Rate of NO₂ photolysis = rate of NO reformation (other pathways ignored):**

$$ J_{NO_2}[NO_2] = k_1[NO][O_3] $$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain Leighton Ratio:**

$$ \phi = \frac{[NO][O_3]}{[NO_2]} = \frac{J_{NO_2}}{k_1} $$

> **Significance for EIA:** When VOC concentrations rise, the Leighton Ratio is perturbed, and ozone accumulates far above equilibrium. This is the *fundamental* reason urban airsheds need VOC control in addition to NOₓ control.

### 3.3 Derivation: pH of Acid Rain from a Single SO₄ Source

**Given:** A cloud contains 5 mg/L of dissolved SO₄²⁻ from oxidised SO₂.

**Step 1 — Molarity of sulphate:**

$$ M = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3} \; g/L}{96 \; g/mol} = 5.208 \times 10^{-5} \; mol/L $$

**Step 2 — Each sulphate ion yields 2 H⁺ upon dissociation:**

$$ [H^+] = 2 \times 5.208 \times 10^{-5} = 1.0417 \times 10^{-4} \; mol/L $$

**Step 3 — Calculate pH:**

$$ \text{pH} = -\log_{10}(1.0417 \times 10^{-4}) = 3.98 $$

> **Interpretation:** Pure rainwater pH ≈ 5.6 (natural CO₂ equilibrium). pH 3.98 is **~40 times more acidic** than natural rain — characteristic of severe acid rain episodes documented in **Kerala (Silent Valley)** and **NE India** during winter.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Primary vs Secondary Pollutants

| **Parameter** | **Primary Pollutants** | **Secondary Pollutants** |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Direct emission point | Atmospheric chemical reaction |
| Persistence (residence time) | Short to medium (minutes–days) | Medium to long (days–weeks) |
| Spatial extent | Local / near-field | Regional / transboundary |
| Regulatory control | At the source (stack, tailpipe) | At multiple precursor sources |
| Examples | CO, SO₂, NO, PM₁₀, Pb | O₃, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, PAN, NH₄NO₃ |
| Measurement methodology | Continuous Emission Monitoring (CEMS) | Ambient air monitoring + receptor modelling |
| EIA modelling tool | Emission Factor / Mass Balance | AERMOD photochemical, CALPUFF, CMAQ |
| Mitigation cost | Moderate (end-of-pipe) | Very high (regional coordination) |
| KTU Exam weightage | High (definitions, examples) | High (chemistry, smog formation) |

### 3.5 Formation Pathways of Common Secondary Pollutants

| **Secondary Pollutant** | **Precursors** | **Reaction Trigger** | **Environmental Impact** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tropospheric O₃ | NOₓ + VOCs | Sunlight (UV ≤ 430 nm) | Respiratory illness, crop damage |
| H₂SO₄ (acid rain) | SO₂ + OH· / H₂O₂ | Photo-oxidation | Lake acidification, stone corrosion |
| HNO₃ (acid rain) | NO₂ + OH· | Photo-oxidation | Soil leaching of Ca, Mg, K |
| PAN | VOCs + NO₂ | Sunlight | Eye irritation, phytotoxicity |
| Secondary PM₂.� | SO₄²⁻, NO₃⁻, NH₄⁺, SOA | Gas-to-particle conversion | Haze, cardiovascular mortality |
| Formaldehyde | CH₄ / VOCs + OH· | Atmospheric oxidation | Carcinogen (Group 1, IARC) |

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Describe the major **primary air pollutants** emitted from a coal-based thermal power plant, with their formation chemistry. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)** — Coal contains 0.5 %–5 % sulphur. On combustion, $S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$. Each kg of S produces 2 kg of SO₂. (2 marks)
2. **Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ)** — At flame temperatures > 1300 °C, atmospheric N₂ + O₂ → 2 NO (Zeldovich mechanism). (2 marks)
3. **Particulate Matter (PM)** — Fly ash, soot, and unburnt carbon released mechanically and by condensation. (1.5 marks)
4. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** — From incomplete combustion when O₂ supply is deficient. (1 mark)
5. **VOCs** — Volatilisation of hydrocarbons present in coal and lubricants. (0.5 marks)

---

**(b)** Discuss the **secondary pollutants** formed in the atmosphere from these primary emissions, with balanced chemical equations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Sulphuric Acid (H₂SO₄)** — Acid rain component.

$$ \text{SO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_3, \quad \text{SO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \quad (2 \text{ marks}) $$

2. **Nitric Acid (HNO₃)** —

$$ 4\text{NO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\text{HNO}_3 \quad (1.5 \text{ marks}) $$

3. **Ground-level Ozone (O₃)** —

$$ \text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}, \quad \text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_3 \quad (2 \text{ marks}) $$

4. **PAN and Secondary PM** — VOCs + NO₂ + sunlight → PAN; NH₃ + HNO₃ → NH₄NO₃ aerosol. (1.5 marks)

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** What is **photochemical smog**? Explain its formation with the **Leighton Ratio** and list four of its effects on human health and environment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Photochemical smog is a mixture of pollutants formed by the photochemical reaction of NOₓ and VOCs in the presence of sunlight, producing ozone, PAN, aldehydes, and fine particulates.

**Leighton Ratio Derivation (3 marks):**

$$ \phi = \frac{[NO][O_3]}{[NO_2]} = \frac{J_{NO_2}}{k_1} $$

The photostationary state breaks down when VOCs are present, because VOCs regenerate NO₂ and shift equilibrium, allowing O₃ to accumulate above equilibrium.

**Effects (3 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. Eye/throat irritation (PAN, formaldehyde).
2. Pulmonary oedema and reduced lung function (O₃).
3. Crop yield reduction (PAN damages leafy vegetables).
4. Visibility reduction (secondary aerosols scatter light).
5. Material damage (rubber cracking, polymer degradation).

---

**(b)** With suitable equations, explain the formation of **acid rain**. Discuss the role of **pH scale** in quantifying its severity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Acid rain is precipitation (rain, fog, snow) with pH < 5.6 caused by dissolution of H₂SO₄ and HNO₃ formed from atmospheric SO₂ and NOₓ.

2. **SO₂ Pathway (2 marks):**

$$ \text{SO}_2 \xrightarrow{O_2, H_2O} \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \quad \text{(via SO}_3\text{ or aqueous H}_2\text{SO}_3\text{)} $$

3. **NOₓ Pathway (2 marks):**

$$ 2\text{NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{HNO}_3 + \text{HNO}_2 $$

4. **pH Quantification (2 marks):**

$$ \text{pH} = -\log_{10}[H^+] $$

- Natural rain pH = 5.6 (CO₂ equilibrium)
- pH 4.0 = 40× more acidic than natural
- pH 3.0 = 400× more acidic

Lower pH = greater ecosystem damage; aquatic life dies below pH 4.5; marble and limestone structures corrode visibly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Avoid These Mistakes:**
>
> 1. **Confusing tropospheric O₃ with stratospheric O₃** — O₃ at ground level is a *pollutant*; O₃ in the stratosphere is *protective*. Examiners deduct 1 mark if confused.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of sunlight (hν)** — Photolysis of NO₂ is impossible without specifying $h\nu$ with $\lambda \le 430$ nm. Always include this in the equation.
> 3. **Writing incomplete equations** — $H_2SO_4$ formation *must* show both the oxidation of SO₂ → SO₃ and the hydration step.
> 4. **Missing the "third body M"** — In O₃ formation, the body (N₂ or O₂) carries away the excess energy of recombination; without it the reaction is endothermic. ½ mark deduction if omitted.
> 5. **Mixing up PAN with PBN** — Per**a**cetyl nitrate (CH₃COOONO₂) is PAN; peroxy**b**enzoyl nitrate (C₆H₅COOONO₂) is PBN. Students sometimes swap the abbreviations.
> 6. **Not linking secondary pollutants to EIA** — Answers that only give chemistry without showing *why it matters in impact assessment* lose the "application" marks (1–2 marks lost).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Module 1 Revision — Print This Box**

- **Primary Pollutants**: Emitted **directly** (CO, SO₂, NO, NO₂, PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, VOCs, Pb, NH₃).
- **Secondary Pollutants**: Formed **in atmosphere** (O₃, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, PAN, SOA, formaldehyde).
- **Photochemical Smog Key Reaction**: $\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}$, followed by $\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}$.
- **Acid Rain Key Reactions**: SO₂ → H₂SO₄ (acid), NO₂ → HNO₃ (acid); natural rain pH = 5.6, polluted pH often < 4.5.
- **PAN** = CH₃COOONO₂ (peroxyacetyl nitrate) — photochemical oxidant; eye irritant; phytotoxic.
- **S to SO₂ mass ratio** = 2.0; useful for emission inventory.
- **ppb to µg/m³ conversion**: $\mu g/m^3 = ppb \times M/24.45$ (at 25 °C, 1 atm).
- **Leighton Ratio** = $\phi = J_{NO_2}/k_1$ — photostationary equilibrium indicator.
- **NAAQS pollutants (India, 2009)**: PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, Pb, NH₃, Benzene, BaP, As, Ni.
- **AERMOD / CALPUFF** — Models used in EIA for primary dispersion; **CMAQ / WRF-Chem** — Models for secondary photochemistry.
- **KTU-favourite terms**: Receptor, AQI, NAAQS, photostationary state, third-body M, Zeldovich mechanism, transboundary pollution, SLCFs.
- **EIA Relevance**: Control of **precursors** (NOₓ, VOCs, SO₂) is the only way to control **secondary** pollutants — end-of-pipe controls on O₃ or PAN are infeasible.
- **Memorise 4–5 balanced equations** — they fetch 6–8 marks alone in Part B.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Air Pollutants on Human, Vegetation and Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT617 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Impact of Air Pollutants on Human, Vegetation and Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Air Pollutants? (Formal KTU Definition)

Air pollutants are defined by the **World Health Organization (WHO)** and the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), India** as any substance in the atmosphere, whether in solid, liquid, or gaseous form, in concentrations or durations above their natural ambient levels, that can cause harm to human health, living resources, ecosystems, climate, materials, or property.

Under the **Indian National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS, 2009)**, twelve core "criteria pollutants" are regulated:

$$\text{Criteria Pollutants} = \{PM_{2.5},\ PM_{10},\ SO_2,\ NO_2,\ CO,\ O_3,\ Pb,\ NH_3,\ C_6H_6,\ BaP,\ Ni,\ As\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The student must be able to identify the specific receptor (Human / Vegetation / Environment) for each pollutant and link it to the correct **adverse end-point**. Examiners repeatedly test the *receptor-to-impact mapping* skill.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Kitchen"

Imagine your atmosphere as a giant, open-plan kitchen where **factories, vehicles, and thermal power plants are the chefs constantly cooking without an exhaust hood**. The "smoke" they release is not just visible soot — it includes invisible gases (CO, SO₂, NO₂), microscopic particles (PM₂.₅), and reactive chemicals (VOCs, ozone).

- **The Kitchen Walls (Vegetation):** absorb the gases just like wallpaper absorbs grease — eventually they stain, wilt, or peel.
- **The Cooks (Humans):** are forced to inhale this contaminated air, developing "smoker's lungs" even if they never touched a cigarette.
- **The Building Itself (Environment):** suffers corrosion (acid rain), faded paint (ozone damage), and a rising room temperature (global warming).

This is exactly what happens on a planetary scale — a phenomenon rigorously quantified in **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** under the **EIA Notification, 2006** (amended 2020).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Ambient breathable oxygen | $O_2$ | **20.9 %** by volume |
| Carboxyhaemoglobin toxicity threshold | COHb | **> 2.5 %** causes impairment |
| Reference concentration (CO, 8-hr) | $C_{ref}$ | **9 ppm (10 mg/m³)** NAAQS |
| Fine particulate cutoff | $PM_{2.5}$ | aerodynamic diameter $\leq$ **2.5 µm** |
| Coarse particulate cutoff | $PM_{10}$ | aerodynamic diameter $\leq$ **10 µm** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AQI vs. Concentration:** Concentration is what is *measured* (µg/m³ or ppm); AQI is the *derived non-linear index* (0–500) that translates raw concentration into a public-health risk category. KTU questions frequently mix these two up — **do not**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Pollutant → Receptor" Impact Pathway

Air pollution impact follows a deterministic three-stage cascade. Every KTU answer on this topic should explicitly trace this pathway:

**Step 1 — Emission:** Pollutant is released from a stack, vent, or area source.

**Step 2 — Dispersion & Transformation:** Buoyancy, wind speed ($u$), atmospheric stability class (Pasquill–Gifford A–F), and photochemistry alter concentration. Secondary pollutants like $O_3$ and $HNO_3$ are *formed in the atmosphere itself*, not emitted directly.

**Step 3 — Receptor Uptake:** Human lungs, leaf stomata, building materials, and water bodies act as receptors. The **dose received** depends on:
- Concentration ($C$, in µg/m³)
